mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in real GDP indicates a robust economic expansion, which supports corporate earnings and investor confidence, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over a longer horizon.
failure_condition: If the annualized quarter-over-quarter growth rate of GDPC1 is positive, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 60-day returns in more than 40% of such instances.

mechanism_narrative: A significant increase in the unemployment rate signals a weakening labor market and potential economic contraction, which typically leads to reduced consumer spending and corporate profits, driving negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises by more than 0.3 percentage points over the prior 3 months.
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day returns are positive in more than 50% of periods where UNRATE rises by >0.3pp over 3 months, or the average return is not statistically different from zero.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate is rising while inflation (CPIAUCSL) remains elevated, it indicates a hawkish monetary policy stance aimed at curbing inflation, which increases borrowing costs and reduces economic activity, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS has increased for 3 consecutive months and CPIAUCSL is above 2.5% year-over-year.
regime_hint: monetary tightening cycle with persistent inflation
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day returns are positive in more than 45% of periods where FEDFUNDS is rising for 3 months and CPIAUCSL > 2.5% YoY, or the average return is not significantly negative.

mechanism_narrative: Robust industrial production growth indicates strong manufacturing and supply-side activity, which translates into higher corporate revenues and profits, supporting positive S&P 500 forward returns, especially during periods of economic expansion.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO 3-month annualized growth rate is above 3%.
failure_condition: If INDPRO growth > 3% annualized does not lead to positive 60-day S&P 500 returns in at least 60% of instances during identified expansionary regimes.

mechanism_narrative: A sudden surge in housing starts (HOUST) indicates strong consumer and business confidence in the real estate sector, suggesting future economic activity and demand, which can provide a short-term boost to S&P 500 returns.
candidate_trigger: HOUST increases by more than 10% month-over-month.
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day returns are negative in more than 50% of periods following a >10% MoM increase in HOUST, or the average return is not statistically positive.

mechanism_narrative: Consistent job creation, as measured by non-farm payrolls, reflects a healthy and growing labor market, which underpins consumer spending and overall economic stability, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: If the 3-month average change in PAYEMS is positive, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 40% of such instances.

mechanism_narrative: When retail sales (RSAFS) are declining while inflation (CPIAUCSL) remains high, it suggests stagflationary pressures where consumer demand is weakening despite rising prices, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a longer horizon.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS 3-month annualized growth rate is negative and CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth is above 3%.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day returns are positive in more than 40% of periods where RSAFS is declining and CPIAUCSL > 3% YoY, or the average return is not significantly negative.

mechanism_narrative: An unexpectedly high Federal Funds Rate, relative to its recent trend, signals a more aggressive monetary stance than anticipated, which can shock markets by increasing discount rates and tightening financial conditions, leading to immediate negative S&P 500 returns.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS is 25 basis points higher than its 3-month moving average.
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day returns are positive in more than 55% of instances where FEDFUNDS unexpectedly rises, or the average return is not statistically negative.
